title: ICHA 2025. Scientific Highlights
author: Murray, Shauna
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: The 21st International Conference on Harmful Algae (ICHA 2025) in Punta Arenas, Chile, highlighted advancements in harmful algal bloom (HAB) research driven by innovative technologies such as remote sensing, in situ sensors, Imaging FlowCytoBot (IFCB), quantitative PCR (qPCR), metabarcoding, and omics analyses. Presentations emphasized integrated approaches combining ecological, oceanographic, technological, and socio-economic perspectives for effective HAB management. Citizen science, involving shellfish farmers and indigenous communities, was prominent, utilizing low-cost tools alongside biosensors, environmental DNA (eDNA), and passive samplers. The conference, attended by 345 participants from 46 countries, underscored climate change impacts on HAB dynamics, including shifts in species distributions and bloom timings. Ecology sessions featured studies on cyst germination influenced by warming, with examples from  Alexandrium catenella  in various regions showing variable responses to temperature. Novel mitigation strategies and the classification of HABs as natural disasters were discussed, reflecting their socio-economic effects. Overall, ICHA 2025 revealed ongoing surprises in HAB occurrences and the need for interdisciplinary collaboration.

keywords: Harmful Algal Blooms, ICHA 2025, climate change, citizen science, remote sensing, Alexandrium catenella, cyst germination, mitigation technologies, interdisciplinary approaches, plankton imaging
